metadata.purpose: Lactarius, Lactifluus, Russula, and closely related brittle-gilled records.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Recognition and comparison only. Do not consume from this entry.
records.general_notes: Useful as a group entry because amateurs often meet the field concept before they sort out the exact Russula name. Flexible gills are a meaningful criterion here. Representative Commons images use Russula cyanoxantha to illustrate the mild flexible-gill charcoal-burner group.
records.food_quality: Source review / not recommended from this app entry

research_notes: MushroomExpert supports the Russula cyanoxantha / flexible-gill field concept for ID but does not clear edible use.
Broad Russula food routing needs stronger edible-use support and caution copy before review can be cleared.

records.image_coverage_note: Exact public-usable image not currently verified; visible images removed pending exact file-page rights metadata.
records.image_rights_notes: Visible Commons image(s) removed in v4.3.176 because stored metadata did not include exact file-page author/license/license-url rights details; do not restore until exact rights are verified.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Russula cyanoxantha
use_links.notes: Supports the Russula cyanoxantha / flexible-gill field concept, including flexible/greasy gills, mild taste, white spore print, and North American species-complex caution; this is ID support only, not edible-use clearance.

records.edibility_detail: Recognition-only HOLD. Exact species-group treatment and safety clearance remain unresolved.
records.mushroom_card_note: Flexible-gilled white-spored Russula concept; exact species and edible use remain under review.
records.safety: Recognition-only / source review needed; not a food target from this entry
records.risk_reason: Broad Russula-group food framing is not source-cleared here. Mild taste is not enough by itself; confirm no ring, no volva/cup, no latex, white-to-cream spore print, and reject acrid/hot/burning Russulas.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, and source-review context rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
source_review_notes: v4.3.179 normalized this broad Russula group to ID/caution because current active sources support identification only, not edible-use clearance.
Future food routing would require a strong edible-use source for the Russula cyanoxantha / mild flexible-gill concept plus caution framing for Russula ID difficulty.
MushroomExpert supports the flexible/greasy young gills, mottled purple-green cap, white print, mild taste, and North American species-complex warning.
Broad Russula food routing remains rejected until a strong edible-use source and safer species/group framing are available; exact image rights are also still missing.
records.scientific_name: Russula cyanoxantha group / flexible-gilled Russula complex
records.overview: Recognition record for the Russula cyanoxantha flexible-gill field concept: mottled purple, green, olive, or grayish caps; white print; mild taste; and unusually flexible, greasy-feeling young gills. North American material may represent more than one species.
records.field_identification: Confirm a brittle white stem and flesh, no ring or volva, white attached gills that are flexible and greasy-feeling when young, a white spore print, mild taste only after expert confirmation, and a purple-green-olive mottled cap that may show fine radial veins.

records.notes: MushroomExpert explicitly treats North American R. brevipes as a species-complex problem, with multiple cryptic forms and variable taste/ecology. Group treatment is safer.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Usually not a top table mushroom on its own; better known as a host for lobster mushroom. Use only firm, mild-tasting specimens after confident ID.
records.general_notes: A good reminder that russulas belong in the gilled lane too. The short thick stem and brittle white flesh matter more than generic 'mushroom' features.

cap_color: Large, sturdy, white to off-white, often funnel-shaped/depressed; cap skin not easily peeled.
gill_color: White to cream; attached to decurrent; spacing variable; brittle Russula gills that do not exude latex.
stem_color: Short, thick, white, sturdy; no ring, volva, or latex.
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: No latex. This is the key separator from white Lactarius look-alikes.
staining: White to off-white cap often becomes tan, brownish, or orange-brown with age; gills/stem may bruise brownish.
smell: Usually mild/not decisive; old specimens may be unpleasant.
taste: Group varies from mild to acrid. Do not treat all short-stemmed white Russulas as edible just because they look like R. brevipes.
host_trees: hardwood/conifer associations vary across the complex
host_filter_tokens: hardwood/conifer associations vary across the complex
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to creamy; group members may reach pale cream. Use a sufficiently thick print, and do not treat color alone as species-level proof.

records.field_identification: Russula-style entry: brittle/chalky flesh and no latex when cut. Large, sturdy, white to off-white, often funnel-shaped/depressed; cap skin not easily peeled. White to cream; attached to decurrent; spacing variable; brittle Russula gills that do not exude latex. Short, thick, white, sturdy; no ring, volva, or latex.
records.mushroom_card_note: Conservative white-russula entry; useful mainly as a lobster-mushroom host.

records.edibility_detail: Advanced/conditional edible posture at most; many are mediocre and some are acrid. Best app use: host/look-alike education and no-latex separator.
records.look_alike_notes: Lactarius deceptivus: near-identical at a glance but exudes white latex when cut cut flesh/gills; do not rely on shape White peppery milkcaps: white latex and often crowded gills/acrid burn not beginner edibles Destroying angel / white Amanita group: free gills, ring, volva/cup at base; no brittle Russula texture deadly; inspect base Lobster mushroom host forms: when parasitized by Hypomyces, host identity can be obscured by orange crust covered under lobster mushroom entry, not raw R. brevipes ID

edible_use.notes: Advanced/conditional edible posture at most; many are mediocre and some are acrid. Best app use: host/look-alike education and no-latex separator.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

lookalike_risk.label: High — Russula/milkcap/Amanita group confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Russula food use needs brittle flesh, no latex, no ring/volva, mild/non-acrid traits, and group-specific confirmation.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Latex, Amanita base/ring traits, or acrid taste means do not eat as edible Russula.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Acrid Russulas are not edible basket mushrooms.
Latex means it is not a Russula entry.
Any Amanita base/ring traits mean do not eat.

records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds a limited group-level spore-print description and normalized filters; finer separation remains review-sensitive and may require a thick print or microscopy.

records.medicinal_uses: Primarily culinary/ID interest in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Use only mild-tasting, confidently identified shrimp russulas. Cook and sample conservatively; the fishy/shrimp odor and staining pattern are key clues, not permission to guess.

cap_color: Variable red, wine-red, purple, brownish, or mixed tones depending on group member; cap color alone is not enough.
gill_color: White to cream/yellowish, brittle, attached; no latex.
stem_color: White to flushed; may discolor brownish/yellowish with age or handling; no ring or volva.
staining: Flesh can slowly discolor yellowish brown to brown when sliced; odor becomes more fishy/shrimp-like with age or drying.
smell: Fishy or shrimp-like, often faint when young and stronger with age or drying.
taste: Mild taste is part of the group concept. Reject hot, peppery, or acrid red/purple Russulas.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Creamy to yellowish or orangish-yellow, varying by group member. Use a sufficiently thick print; microscopy is generally needed for species-level separation.

records.scientific_name: North American Russula xerampelina group / shrimp Russulas
records.field_identification: Russula-style entry: brittle/chalky flesh and no latex when cut. Variable red, wine-red, purple, brownish, or mixed tones depending on group member; cap color alone is not enough. White to cream/yellowish, brittle, attached; no latex. White to flushed; may discolor brownish/yellowish with age or handling; no ring or volva.
records.mushroom_card_note: Edible russula group, but do not wing it: confirm mild taste, brittle russula structure, and shrimp/fishy odor.

records.edibility_detail: Edible group for advanced confirmation only. Use this entry only when it shows mild taste, fish/shrimp odor developing with age/drying, no latex, and Russula brittleness. Not a beginner color-match entry.
records.notes: MushroomExpert treats shrimp Russulas as a North American group with many members. Group treatment is much safer than a single R. xerampelina species label.
records.look_alike_notes: Acrid red Russula / sickener group: hot/acrid taste and no shrimp/fishy odor pattern GI poison; avoid acrid Russulas Other red/purple Russulas: lack shrimp/fishy odor, may have different spore print and staining behavior color is a lousy steering wheel here

edible_use.notes: Edible group for advanced confirmation only. Use this entry only when it shows mild taste, fish/shrimp odor developing with age/drying, no latex, and Russula brittleness. Not a beginner color-match entry.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.quality_notes: Very variable; some are brittle, crumbly, bland, peppery, or buggy.
records.eating_use_notes: Use only species/groups with known safe posture; cook thoroughly.
records.risk_reason: Russula is too broad for casual app confidence. Acrid/spicy Russulas can cause GI distress, and color is unreliable.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract; nausea, vomiting, stomach pain, diarrhea, and dehydration risk in vulnerable people.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Brittle/chalky flesh, no latex, many cap colors, and difficult species-level taxonomy. Red/purple color alone is not a safe ID trait.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat Russulas unless the exact edible species/group is confirmed. Do not eat red, acrid, peppery, or bitter Russulas.

lookalike_risk.label: High — Russula/milkcap/Amanita group confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Russula food use needs brittle flesh, no latex, no ring/volva, mild/non-acrid traits, and group-specific confirmation.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Latex, Amanita base/ring traits, or acrid taste means do not eat as edible Russula.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Acrid Russulas are not edible basket mushrooms.
Latex means it is not a Russula entry.
Any Amanita base/ring traits mean do not eat.

records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds a limited group-level spore-print description and normalized filters; finer separation remains review-sensitive and may require a thick print or microscopy.

records.edibility_detail: Edible-with-caution only when a specimen fits the mild volemus/hygrophoroides-type combination. This record does not authorize eating milkcaps generally. Reject hot, bitter, strongly peppery, yellowing-latex, blue-latex, orange-latex, watery-latex, old, dry, or otherwise uncertain specimens.
records.culinary_uses: Some confirmed volemus/hygrophoroides-type milkcaps are used as food after cooking. Keep this as an advanced-identification target rather than a beginner color match.
records.overview: Edible-with-caution field group limited to the orange-brown, white-latex volemus/hygrophoroides complex. It is not a catch-all edible category for milkcaps.
records.notes: This record is intentionally limited to volemus- and hygrophoroides-type milkcaps. Indigo and orange-latex milkcaps have separate records, while acrid, yellowing, woolly, watery-latex, and poorly identified milkcaps remain outside this edible group.
records.general_notes: Edible-with-caution field group limited to the orange-brown, white-latex volemus/hygrophoroides complex. It is not a catch-all edible category for milkcaps.
records.field_identification: Orange-brown to cinnamon-orange milkcaps with copious white latex. Volemus-type mushrooms have close creamy gills, a fishy odor that strengthens after picking, and latex/cut tissue that browns. Hygrophoroides-type mushrooms have distinctly distant gills, little or no fishy odor, and latex that usually remains white or changes only slightly. Confirm the exact combination before treating a specimen as food.

cap_color: Brownish orange to cinnamon orange; generally unzoned
gill_color: Creamy white to cream or pale yellowish; damaged areas may brown
stem_color: Colored like the cap or paler
latex_color_change: Volemus-type: may become brownish and stains tissue, hands, and white paper brown
Hygrophoroides-type: usually unchanging or only slightly yellowish; may stain white paper yellow overnight
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: The exact staining pattern and gill spacing separate the two sides of this group.
staining: Volemus-type flesh and damaged gills slowly brown
Hygrophoroides-type may remain unstained or pass through lavender to brown in some collections
smell: Fishy in volemus-type mushrooms, stronger after picking
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North America; hygrophoroides is especially associated with summer.
cap_surface: May become slightly wrinkled or corrugated with age
flesh_color: White; slowly browning when cut in volemus-type mushrooms

records.look_alike_notes: Close gills plus fishy odor and brown-staining white latex point toward the volemus side of this group; distant gills, little odor, and white latex with little staining point toward hygrophoroides. Blue latex, orange latex, watery latex, rapidly yellowing white latex, woolly margins, or an acrid burn belong in other milkcap records. Russulas can look similar but do not exude latex.

edible_use.notes: Advanced-identification food group limited to mild volemus/hygrophoroides-type milkcaps. Cook before eating and try only a small amount of a confidently identified specimen.

records.image_needs: Needs fresh cut gills showing latex, same cut after 30 seconds/5 minutes/30–60 minutes, latex staining paper/tissue, cap zoning/scrobicules, gill spacing comparison, and host/habitat images.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Old/dry specimens may not produce latex; do not force an ID from dried-out material. Reject moldy, waterlogged, sour-smelling, insect-heavy, or weakly identified specimens. Avoid acrid/hot milk caps unless the app has a specific, regionally appropriate edible treatment.
records.quality_notes: Quality varies by species; some are good, some grainy, acrid, bitter, or poor.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed edible milkcap groups. Do not use generic latex as permission to eat.
records.risk_reason: The old broad record could be read as though milkcaps generally were edible. This narrower group requires white latex, mild taste, and the volemus/hygrophoroides character set.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Some toxic or irritating milkcaps can cause gastrointestinal and autonomic symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, salivation, sweating, vertigo, or cold feeling.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Latex may be white, colored, watery, scant, or absent in old/dry specimens. Staining reactions can take up to an hour. Acrid, bitter, strongly peppery, or uncertain milkcaps should not be eaten.
records.hard_rule: Do not use this record for milkcaps generally. Only consider specimens that fit the mild, white-latex volemus/hygrophoroides group; reject acrid, colored-latex, rapidly yellowing, watery-latex, or uncertain specimens.
records.caution_reason: Group complexity and latex/staining/taste/host-tree dependence; caution is ID risk, not food value.

lookalike_risk.label: High — milkcap group complexity / acrid look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — milkcap group complexity / acrid look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Volemus-type edible milk caps need white latex, correct odor/staining behavior, host/habitat, and mild taste under expert protocol. Old dry specimens can lose reliable latex.
required_checks: Reject acrid, peppery, woolly, yellowing-latex, or unidentified milkcaps
Old dry specimens with unreliable latex are not beginner-safe
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat milk caps based only on “it has latex”; exact milkcap type must be confirmed.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Peppery white milkcaps stay out of edible milkcap use.

target_traits: Volemus-type white latex behavior, not quick sulfur-yellow change
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Yellowing latex means do not eat as volemus-type milkcap.
Woolly pink acrid milkcaps are not edible milkcaps.
No latex in a fresh specimen means re-check as Russula, not milkcap.

records.image_coverage_note: Verified Volemus-type representative image added; this does not represent all edible milkcaps.

records.notes: This is one of the few milkcaps where a species-level entry is practical: blue fruit body plus blue latex are field-distinctive, with only a few partial look-alikes.
records.field_identification: Deep to medium blue, often concentrically zoned cap; blue gills; deep indigo latex; and green staining on damaged or aging tissue. The cap is sticky or slimy when fresh, the gills are close and attached to slightly decurrent, and the stem is often pitted and becomes hollow with age.

cap_color: Deep to medium blue when fresh; fading gray or silvery blue; often concentrically zoned
gill_color: Blue to paler blue; becoming nearly yellowish at maturity; staining green
latex_abundance: Variable; older or dry specimens may produce little latex
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Deep blue latex is the central field character; blue-gray mushrooms without blue latex are not this species.
staining: Bruised and aging surfaces become deep green
stem_features: Solid when young and hollowing with age
flesh_color: Whitish, turning indigo blue when cut, then slowly greenish

records.look_alike_notes: Lactarius paradoxus: can be blue-gray when young but has reddish-brown to purple-brown latex/gills rather than true blue latex do not treat as L. indigo without blue latex Lactarius chelidonium: yellowish/dingy cap tones and yellowish to brown latex, not indigo-blue milk separate; avoid uncertain blue-gray milkcaps
records.edibility_detail: Generally treated as edible, though texture/flavor quality varies. App posture should be: edible only with unmistakable blue latex and blue/green-staining Lactarius traits.

edible_use.notes: Generally treated as edible, though texture/flavor quality varies. App posture should be: edible only with unmistakable blue latex and blue/green-staining Lactarius traits.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.quality_notes: Distinctive and useful, but usually not top-tier table quality.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook; slice thin if firm and expect blue staining of pan/food.
records.risk_reason: Risk is mostly correct ID within Lactarius and avoiding broad “blue mushroom = edible” thinking.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Must have indigo-blue latex and blue/green staining Lactarius traits, not merely a bluish cap.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat uncertain blue-gray milkcaps without blue latex.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — confirm actual blue latex
lookalike_risk.summary: Indigo milk cap is distinctive only when actual blue latex and blue tissue/staining behavior are confirmed; blue-gray cap color alone is not enough.
required_checks: Cut gills/flesh and confirm deep indigo latex
Confirm blue tissues with greenish staining of damage
Reject old dry specimens if latex/traits are unclear
Do not use cap color alone
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: No blue latex means no indigo milk cap.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: White/yellowing latex is not indigo milk cap.
Orange latex routes to orange-latex milkcap, not indigo.
White peppery milkcaps are not indigo milk caps.

records.scientific_name: North American Lactarius deliciosus-type group, including Lactarius deliciosus var. deterrimus sensu Kuo and Lactarius thyinos
records.notes: Group treatment is safer because the European name Lactarius deliciosus is commonly misapplied in North America, and eastern/northern taxa are still handled inconsistently in guides.
records.field_identification: Orange to salmon-orange, often zoned milkcaps with orange latex and gills attached to decurrent. Many Michigan deliciosus-type collections bruise green or develop dark red staining; Lactarius thyinos, associated especially with northern white cedar, has orange latex but does not bruise green and instead stains tissue red slowly.

cap_color: Carrot orange to salmon orange; often concentrically zoned; green patches may develop in greening taxa
gill_color: Orange to pale orange; bruising green, brown, or red depending on taxon
latex_abundance: Usually evident when fresh gills are cut
latex_color_change: Lactarius thyinos latex remains orange and slowly stains tissue red without green bruising
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Orange latex is required; white latex that later turns yellow belongs in the yellowing group.
staining: Brown to dull red in Lactarius thyinos
smell: Mild, faintly fragrant, resinous, fruity, or not distinctive
taste: Mild to slightly bitter; reject hot or strongly acrid specimens
host_trees: Northern white cedar for Lactarius thyinos
Other conifers for Michigan deliciosus-type taxa
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in the northeastern United States and Upper Great Lakes region.
cap_surface: Slimy or thinly slimy when fresh
gill_spacing: Close to nearly distant, depending on taxon
stem_features: May have shallow potholes or scrobicules

records.look_alike_notes: Lactarius vinaceorufescens / yellowing milkcaps: white latex that quickly turns yellow, not orange latex avoid; this guide should not place yellow-latex milkcaps in the saffron edible bucket Jack-o'-lantern mushroom: orange true-gilled mushroom on wood/buried roots with no latex poisonous GI mushroom; not a milkcap Chanterelles: false ridges rather than true gills and no latex different morphology lane
records.edibility_detail: Edible group after confident orange-latex confirmation, but not species-level in the app unless a specimen is keyed carefully. Do not use the European L. deliciosus as the primary North American label.

edible_use.notes: Edible group after confident orange-latex confirmation, but not species-level in the app unless a specimen is keyed carefully. Do not use the European L. deliciosus as the primary North American label.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.quality_notes: Some are crumbly, grainy, or green-staining; green staining is often appearance/quality, not danger by itself.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook; good sautéed, fried, braised, or preserved. Use species/group-level confirmation.
records.risk_reason: Milk color, staining, taste, host tree, and ground-growing habit matter. White-latex yellowing milkcaps and orange non-milkcaps should not be folded into this group.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Orange latex must be observed; some taxa green-stain, while Lactarius thyinos-type forms have orange milk without green bruising. No latex means this is not the target group.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat orange mushrooms as saffron milkcaps unless latex is orange and the rest of the milkcap traits fit.

lookalike_risk.label: High — latex color/change and milkcap complexity
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — latex color/change and milkcap complexity
lookalike_risk.summary: Orange-latex milkcaps require orange latex, host/tree context, and staining behavior; white/yellowing or woolly/acrid milkcaps are not in this edible group.
required_checks: Reject white/yellowing latex, peppery white milk, woolly pink caps, and unidentified milkcaps
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: White/yellowing latex or woolly/acrid milkcap traits mean do not eat as orange-latex milkcap.

target_traits: Expected orange/green or non-greening orange-latex pattern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: White/yellowing latex is not orange-latex saffron milkcap.
Peppery white milkcaps do not belong in orange-latex edible use.
Woolly milkcaps stay out of edible orange-latex treatment.
Blue latex routes to indigo milk cap, not orange-latex group.

records.image_scope_note: Representative image only: Lactarius deliciosus group morphology; not a universal ID image for all North American orange-latex milkcaps and latex is not visible in this mature specimen.

records.notes: Regional relevance is strong: MushroomExpert specifically describes it as prolific in northern Michigan. The record emphasizes latex confirmation against Russula brevipes and gill spacing against peppery white milkcaps.
records.field_identification: Very large white to off-white milkcap that becomes tan or brownish and develops rough patches or scales. The young margin is inrolled and softly cottony/leathery. Gills are close to nearly distant rather than extremely crowded. White latex is unchanging but may stain the gills brownish; taste is strongly acrid.

cap_color: White to off-white, soon discoloring tan or brownish
gill_color: Whitish to pale yellowish, becoming cream and dingy tan; usually staining brownish
latex_abundance: Present when fresh; may be reduced in old or dry specimens
latex_color_change: Unchanging; may stain damaged gills brownish
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Latex separates it from Russula brevipes, which does not produce milk.
staining: Cap and stem become tan or brownish; damaged gills may stain brownish
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; notably prolific in northern Michigan.
cap_surface: Soft cottony inrolled margin when young

records.look_alike_notes: Russula brevipes group: very similar at a glance but Russula has no latex when cut knife-cut gills/flesh before any confidence Lactarius piperatus / glaucescens group: much more crowded gills; some members have milk that changes greenish or yellowish acrid white milkcaps are not beginner edibles Destroying angel buttons: Amanita has free gills, ring/veil remnants, and a buried volva/cup at the base; no latex deadly; base inspection mandatory for white gilled mushrooms
records.edibility_detail: Avoid / not recommended. Important as a lobster-mushroom host/look-alike context and as a white milkcap ID training entry.

edible_use.notes: Avoid / not recommended. Important as a lobster-mushroom host/look-alike context and as a white milkcap ID training entry.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

images_structured.title: Deceptive / mammoth milkcap
images_structured.part_or_stage: whole fruiting bodies in moss/oak woods
images_structured.author: Dan Molter / shroomydan
images_structured.credit: Dan Molter / shroomydan, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons / Mushroom Observer
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.

records.taxonomy_note: Also treated as Lactifluus deceptivus in some current taxonomy; image/file page uses Lactarius deceptivus.

records.scientific_name: Lactarius piperatus / Lactarius glaucescens / Lactarius subvellereus group
records.notes: Group treatment is safer because several white eastern milkcaps overlap heavily, and separating them often hinges on gill crowding, latex color change, cap texture, and taste.
records.field_identification: White to off-white milkcaps with a severe peppery/acrid burn. Piperatus- and glaucescens-type mushrooms have very crowded gills and mostly smooth caps; glaucescens latex slowly turns olive or pastel green. Subvellereus-type mushrooms have a finely velvety cap and stem, more distant gills, and white latex that may yellow slightly and spot the gills brown.

cap_color: White to off-white; sometimes aging yellowish or brownish
gill_color: White to pale cream; brown spotting can develop in subvellereus types
latex_color_change: Piperatus-type: remains white or slowly yellows
Glaucescens-type: slowly turns olive to pastel green
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Record the change over minutes and, when useful, on white paper; the delayed reaction separates otherwise similar white milkcaps.
staining: Yellowish flesh and brown-spotted gills in subvellereus types
smell: Usually not distinctive; glaucescens may be fragrant
taste: Extremely acrid or peppery; avoid casual taste testing
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in eastern North American hardwood forests.
cap_surface: Dry and smooth in piperatus/glaucescens types
gill_spacing: Distant to nearly distant in subvellereus subdistans types
flesh_color: White; may slowly yellow in some members

records.look_alike_notes: Lactarius deceptivus: larger roughened/scaly cap and gills not as densely crowded also not a beginner edible Russula brevipes group: no latex; brittle Russula flesh/gills can look extremely similar at first glance Destroying angel / white Amanita group: free gills, ring, volva/cup at base, no latex deadly; never identify white gilled mushrooms casually
records.edibility_detail: Avoid / not recommended. Primarily useful as a field-ID warning group for white milkcaps.

edible_use.notes: Avoid / not recommended. Primarily useful as a field-ID warning group for white milkcaps.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.risk_reason: White peppery/acrid milkcaps are a common milkcap confusion trap and should not be treated as edible milkcaps by general app users.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal irritation is possible from acrid or improperly identified milkcaps.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat white milkcaps that taste hot, peppery, or acrid; peppery white milkcaps stay out of the edible milkcap bucket.

images_structured.title: Peppery milkcap representative
images_structured.credit: Brian Adamo / adamo588, via Mushroom Observer and Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0
images_structured.author: Brian Adamo / adamo588
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.

records.image_coverage_note: Verified representative image added; this is labeled as representative coverage, not an exact image for every member of the group.

records.notes: Use group treatment for app safety because hairy pink/orange birch milkcaps can be confused, and the practical warning is more important than splitting every close species.
records.field_identification: Pink to salmon-pink zoned cap with an inrolled woolly or shaggy margin, crowded pale pink gills, and white to cream latex that does not change color. The taste is strongly hot/acrid and the mushroom is associated with birch.

cap_color: Pink to salmon pink, usually with darker concentric zones
latex_abundance: Usually visible when fresh gills are damaged
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: White unchanging latex plus the woolly pink margin and birch association are the key combination.
staining: No important latex color change; cap zoning fades with age
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer and fall in damp birch habitat.
cap_surface: Woolly or hairy, especially at the inrolled margin

records.look_alike_notes: Orange-latex milkcaps: orange latex rather than white latex; often conifer/cedar bog association do not judge by orange-pink cap alone Mild edible milkcaps: lack woolly pink margin and acrid burn woolly/acrid milkcaps should be treated as avoid
records.edibility_detail: Poisonous/avoid in this guide. Some traditions process it heavily, but that is outside this guide’s practical safety lane.

edible_use.notes: Poisonous/avoid in this guide. Some traditions process it heavily, but that is outside this guide’s practical safety lane.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.risk_reason: Woolly pinkish milkcaps with white latex and hot/acrid taste are not beginner edible milkcaps and may cause significant gastrointestinal upset.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract: nausea, vomiting, severe diarrhea, abdominal cramps/colic, and dehydration risk in severe cases.
records.hard_rule: Avoid pink woolly milkcaps with white latex and a hot/acrid burn; they are not beginner edible milkcaps and can cause severe gastrointestinal upset.

images_structured.title: Woolly milkcap representative
images_structured.credit: Aleksandar Đukić, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0
images_structured.author: Aleksandar Đukić
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.

records.image_coverage_note: Verified group-level representative image added; not regionally ideal, but usable as labeled representative coverage.

records.notes: North American use of the names Lactarius helvus and L. aquifluus remains taxonomically unsettled. The app therefore keeps a bog/conifer group centered on watery latex, a long stem, and the strong curry or burned-maple odor.
records.field_identification: Brownish, orangish brown, or pinkish brown bog milkcap with a relatively long stem, scant watery latex that does not stain tissue, close attached-to-decurrent gills, and a strong curry, fenugreek, or burned maple-syrup odor that becomes strongest as the mushroom dries.

cap_color: Light brown, orangish brown, or pinkish brown
gill_color: Whitish becoming dirty yellowish; not spotting or staining
stem_color: Orangish, pinkish, tan, or orangish brown
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Watery scant latex separates this group from white-latex weeping milkcaps and orange-latex saffron milkcaps.
staining: No important tissue staining; odor intensifies as specimens dry
smell: Strong curry, fenugreek, burned maple syrup, or maple sugar, especially on drying
taste: Mild to slowly slightly acrid; tasting is not recommended for app users
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall in wet sphagnum and boggy conifer habitat.
spore_print: Creamy white to pale orangish yellow
cap_surface: Smooth or finely velvety when young
Becoming rough or sub-scaly with age
flesh_color: Pale pinkish to pale brownish; becoming dingy with age but not staining

records.look_alike_notes: Lactifluus volemus group: white copious latex that may brown-stain and often fishy odor; not watery latex in sphagnum bog do not confuse food-scent/milk behavior Candy-cap / maple-scented milkcaps: small brown milkcaps with maple odor can overlap by smell; habitat, latex, size, and cap/gill details matter avoid eating scent-only IDs
records.edibility_detail: Avoid / poisonous-risk. Strong smell is not a green light; this guide should warn against treating maple/curry odor as an edible confirmation.

edible_use.notes: Avoid / poisonous-risk. Strong smell is not a green light; this guide should warn against treating maple/curry odor as an edible confirmation.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.

images_structured.title: Fenugreek / burnt-sugar milkcap representative
images_structured.part_or_stage: whole fruiting body; odor not visible and must be confirmed by field traits
images_structured.author: Ryan Hodnett
images_structured.credit: Ryan Hodnett, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons / iNaturalist
images_structured.rights_notes: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.
images_structured.rights_note: Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required and share-alike/copyleft terms may apply to adapted versions.
images_structured.scope_note: Representative for Lactarius helvus / Lactarius aquifluus-type fenugreek/maple-scented milkcap group; not a universal exact ID image.

records.risk_reason: A pleasant maple or curry odor is not an edible confirmation. North American and European concepts are taxonomically messy, and the watery-latex bog group should remain outside normal food routing.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat a maple- or curry-scented milkcap based on odor alone. Watery latex plus bog habitat requires an exact species-level determination beyond this group record.

records.scientific_name: Lactarius vinaceorufescens / Lactarius chrysorrheus-type yellowing milkcaps
records.notes: Practical field rule is stronger than species splitting: white latex that rapidly or slowly yellows is not the same as orange-latex edible saffron milkcaps.
records.field_identification: Milk starts white and turns yellow after exposure, sometimes within seconds. Vinaceorufescens-type mushrooms are cinnamon-pink to reddish, usually zoned and sticky, occur with pines, and develop reddish or brown gill spots. Chrysorrheus-type mushrooms are paler, associated with oaks, and have gills that generally do not spot red.

cap_color: Cinnamon pink to reddish brown in vinaceorufescens types
Pale pinkish to pale cinnamon in chrysorrheus types
gill_color: Pale to cream; vinaceorufescens types develop pinkish, cinnamon, or reddish-brown spots
stem_color: Whitish, becoming cap-colored with age in some taxa
latex_color_change: Turns yellow on exposure, often within seconds
mushroom_profile.latex_notes: Yellowing white latex is a hard separation from the orange-latex saffron group.
staining: Vinaceorufescens-type gills develop pinkish to reddish-brown spotting
host_trees: Pine, especially eastern white pine, for vinaceorufescens types
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; prolific in late summer and fall in northern Michigan conifer woods for vinaceorufescens types.
cap_surface: Sticky or slimy when fresh in vinaceorufescens types
Moist to dry and smooth or finely roughened in chrysorrheus types
stem_features: No potholes in the two core concepts

records.look_alike_notes: Orange-latex milkcaps / saffron group: true orange latex rather than white latex turning yellow do not put yellowing white-latex milkcaps in edible saffron group White peppery milkcaps: white cap and crowded gills; latex may remain white, green, or yellowish by species acrid milkcaps are not beginner edibles
records.edibility_detail: Avoid / not recommended. Use as a warning group attached to orange-latex milkcap searches.

edible_use.notes: Avoid / not recommended. Use as a warning group attached to orange-latex milkcap searches.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.risk_reason: White-latex milkcaps whose milk turns yellow are not edible orange-latex milkcaps and should be treated as avoid in this app.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Primarily gastrointestinal irritation is the practical concern; exact symptoms vary by species and report.
records.hard_rule: Do not put white-latex milkcaps into the edible orange-latex bucket; if the milk starts white and turns yellow, treat it as a poisonous yellowing milkcap and avoid it.

images_structured.part_or_stage: yellowing milkcap fruiting body; yellowing latex trait not shown

records.image_scope_note: Representative image only: Lactarius chrysorrheus / yellowing milkcap morphology. The image does not show active yellowing latex/staining; a better future image should show sulfur-yellow latex or staining, ideally Lactarius vinaceorufescens for North American/Michigan relevance.

records.scientific_name: Russula parvovirescens and related North American green-cracking Russulas formerly treated under Russula virescens concepts
records.notes: Edible green-cracking Russulas are practical, but the old R. virescens concept is taxonomically messy in North America. Group treatment avoids importing European certainty.
records.field_identification: Russula-style entry: brittle/chalky flesh and no latex when cut. Green, blue-green, or gray-green with cracked/quilted patches; often drier and patterned rather than smooth slimy green. White to cream; brittle; attached; no latex. White, brittle/chalky Russula stem; no ring or volva.

cap_color: Green, blue-green, or gray-green with cracked/quilted patches; often drier and patterned rather than smooth slimy green.
gill_color: White to cream; brittle; attached; no latex.
stem_color: White, brittle/chalky Russula stem; no ring or volva.
staining: Generally not a dramatic staining mushroom; cap may crack/quilt and fade with age.
taste: Only mild-tasting green-cracking Russulas belong here. Avoid acrid/hot Russulas.
host_trees: often oak/hardwood settings depending on member
host_filter_tokens: often oak/hardwood settings depending on member
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late June through fall in eastern North American/northern Great Lakes hardwood or mixed woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Creamy for Russula parvovirescens; related green-cracking group members may vary slightly. Use a sufficiently thick print.

records.look_alike_notes: Amanita phalloides-like green Amanitas: Amanitas have ring/volva/free gills and fibrous flesh, not brittle Russula flesh deadly Amanita risk means base/ring/gill attachment must be checked Other green Russulas: may be smooth-capped rather than quilted/cracked; some require spore print/taste/chemistry/microscopy group entry should not overpromise species ID Acrid Russulas: hot/peppery taste, different cap patterns/colors avoid acrid Russulas
records.edibility_detail: Good edible group for advanced foragers when mild and confidently Russula, but keep app posture conservative. Require no ring, no volva, brittle flesh, no latex, and quilted/cracked green cap.

edible_use.notes: Good edible group for advanced foragers when mild and confidently Russula, but keep app posture conservative. Require no ring, no volva, brittle flesh, no latex, and quilted/cracked green cap.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.quality_notes: One of the better green Russula food candidates when mild and clearly quilted/cracked, but quality varies.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed mild specimens. Reject acrid, bitter, or non-Russula look-alikes.
records.risk_reason: Green Russula taxonomy is difficult, and greenish Amanitas are a deadly look-alike category if base/ring/flesh structure are not checked.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Acrid Russulas can cause GI upset; Amanita confusion can affect liver and kidneys and can be fatal.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Requires brittle Russula flesh, no latex, no ring, no volva, and quilted/cracked green cap. Smooth green caps and fibrous Amanita flesh are different problems.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat green Russulas unless brittle Russula structure, no latex, no ring, no volva, and mild taste are confirmed.

lookalike_risk.label: High — Russula/Amanita/acrid Russula confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Green Russulas need brittle Russula traits and no Amanita-style base/ring/volva. Acrid Russulas are a taste/toxicity caution.
required_checks: Confirm brittle, chalky Russula flesh and stem
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Any ring, volva, cup, bulb, latex, or hot/acrid taste means do not eat as green cracking Russula.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Acrid Russulas are not edible basket mushrooms.
Any Amanita-style base or ring means do not eat.

images_structured.author: Dan Molter / shroomydan

records.image_scope_note: Representative image only: Russula parvovirescens, a North American/eastern green-cracking Russula candidate; not exact for every green-cracking Russula.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds a limited group-level spore-print description and normalized filters; finer separation remains review-sensitive and may require a thick print or microscopy.

records.scientific_name: Russula emetica group and similar acrid red Russulas
records.notes: The practical app hazard is red Russulas with acrid/hot taste causing GI illness. Species-level precision is less useful than flagging the avoid group.
records.field_identification: Brittle/chalky Russula flesh; no latex; red to deep red cap; white to pale cream brittle gills; hot, acrid, or peppery taste. Red cap color alone is not enough for ID.

cap_color: Red to deep red, often darker centrally; smooth cap surface; cap skin may peel.
gill_color: White to pale cream, brittle Russula gills; no latex.
stem_color: White or pale, brittle/chalky; no ring or volva.
staining: Often not a strong bruising/staining entry; cap may fade or vary from bright to darker red.
smell: Usually not as useful as taste and habitat; some related red Russulas may have distinctive odors but this guide should not rely on smell alone.
taste: Hot, peppery, acrid, or burning taste is the warning trait. Do not recommend casual taste testing; if expert uses taste, spit out completely.
host_trees: spring through fall in Michigan per MSU source
host_filter_tokens: spring through fall in Michigan per MSU source
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer to fall in northern Great Lakes woods; exact timing varies by species/group.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Varies across warning-group members, generally white to cream or pale yellow. A sufficiently thick print is needed; this broad range is not species-level clearance.

records.look_alike_notes: Shrimp Russula group: mild taste and fishy/shrimp odor with age; not hot/acrid do not eat red Russulas based on color Other mild red Russulas: mild taste, different odor/spore print/ecology; species ID often hard group-level caution is safer Amanita muscaria buttons or red/orange Amanitas: Amanitas have universal veil remnants, ring/volva/basal bulb features; Russulas are brittle and lack veil structures separate dangerous genus check
records.edibility_detail: Poisonous/avoid. Severe gastrointestinal upset is the app message; not deadly in typical North American treatment but absolutely not a food entry.

edible_use.notes: Poisonous/avoid. Severe gastrointestinal upset is the app message; not deadly in typical North American treatment but absolutely not a food entry.

records.image_needs: Needs 2–3 distinct open-license images showing cap/gills/stem plus latex/staining or brittle/no-latex traits as applicable.
records.risk_reason: Acrid red Russulas can be confused with edible Russula groups, but hot/peppery taste is a stop sign and may predict gastrointestinal illness.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and colicky abdominal cramps, often within a few hours.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat red or purple Russulas that taste hot, peppery, or acrid; acrid Russulas belong in the sickener group and can cause vomiting and severe gastrointestinal upset.

records.image_scope_note: Representative image only: Russula emetica-type / sickener morphology; red acrid Russula taxonomy is messy and this is not exact for the entire group.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds only broad group-level spore guidance; it does not clear species-level identification, review debt, or food use.
